title: The Word Patterns That Give Away AI-Generated Text — And Why Detection Is Getting Harder
description: Researchers are using word frequency analysis, sentence burstiness, and phrase repetition to detect AI writing. Here's how it works — and why it's an arms race.
lede: Every writer has a fingerprint. Humans write in bursts — short punchy sentences followed by long meandering ones. AI writes like a metronome. That difference is measurable, and it's the basis of a new generation of detection tools.

sections.heading: The Burstiness Problem
paragraphs: When researchers at Stanford and MIT analyzed millions of text samples in 2025, they found one consistent difference between human and AI writing: burstiness. Human text has high variance in sentence length — a 4-word sentence might follow a 35-word one. AI-generated text clusters tightly around an average, producing sentence after sentence of similar length.

Burstiness scores (the standard deviation of sentence lengths within a text) reliably separate the two. Human-written essays typically score 10-15 on burstiness. GPT-4 output averages 4-7. Claude and Gemini are slightly more varied but still measurably flatter than human writing.

This single metric isn't enough on its own — a skilled writer composing a technical manual might produce low-burstiness text naturally. But combined with other signals, it becomes a powerful indicator.

sections.heading: Vocabulary Diversity and Repetition Patterns
paragraphs: The second major signal is vocabulary diversity — the ratio of unique words to total words. Human writers naturally vary their word choice, especially over longer texts. AI models, despite their enormous vocabularies, tend to fall into repetitive patterns, reusing the same transition words and phrases.

Word frequency analysis reveals these patterns clearly. AI text disproportionately favors words like 'furthermore,' 'moreover,' 'comprehensive,' 'leverage,' 'facilitate,' and 'multifaceted.' The phrase 'it is worth noting' appears in AI output at roughly 50x the rate of human writing. 'Delve into' has become so associated with ChatGPT that its usage has become a meme.

Bigram and trigram analysis (looking at repeated two- and three-word sequences) adds another layer. AI text repeats structural phrases at higher rates than human writing, creating a measurable fingerprint even when individual word choice appears natural.

sections.heading: The Arms Race
paragraphs: Every time a detection technique improves, AI models are updated to evade it. GPT-5's output is significantly harder to detect than GPT-3.5's was — its burstiness scores have crept closer to human ranges, and OpenAI has actively reduced the overuse of flagged phrases.

This has created a cat-and-mouse dynamic that some researchers believe is fundamentally unwinnable. As AI models are trained on more human-written text and fine-tuned to avoid detectable patterns, the statistical differences shrink. The consensus among detection researchers is that no single metric will remain reliable long-term.

The practical implication for schools, publishers, and hiring managers: AI detection tools should be one input in a larger evaluation process, never a sole arbiter. False positives — flagging genuine human writing as AI — remain common, particularly for non-native English speakers whose writing patterns may coincidentally match AI characteristics.

sections.heading: What You Can Actually Do
paragraphs: Despite the limitations, word frequency analysis remains one of the most transparent and understandable detection methods. Unlike black-box AI classifiers that return a probability score with no explanation, frequency analysis shows you exactly what it found — which phrases triggered, how uniform the sentence structure is, and where vocabulary diversity breaks down.

Tools like word frequency analyzers let you inspect text yourself rather than trusting an opaque algorithm. You can see the sentence length distribution, spot repeated bigrams, and check for AI-typical phrases. This makes the analysis auditable and educational, even when the verdict is uncertain.

For writers concerned about their own text being falsely flagged, the advice is straightforward: write naturally, vary your sentence structure, avoid filler phrases, and don't overuse transition words. In other words, the same advice that makes writing better also makes it harder to mistake for AI.

faqs.q: Can AI text detection tools reliably identify ChatGPT output?
faqs.a: No current tool achieves 100% reliability. The best tools combine multiple signals — burstiness, vocabulary diversity, phrase repetition, and perplexity — to reach 85-95% accuracy on unedited AI text. Accuracy drops significantly when AI output is edited by a human.

faqs.q: What is burstiness in text analysis?
faqs.a: Burstiness measures the variance in sentence lengths within a text. Human writing is 'bursty' — mixing very short and very long sentences (score 10+). AI-generated text tends to produce uniform sentence lengths (score 3-7), making it measurably flatter.

faqs.q: What words and phrases are associated with AI-generated text?
faqs.a: Common AI indicators include: 'it is worth noting,' 'delve into,' 'furthermore,' 'moreover,' 'comprehensive,' 'leverage,' 'facilitate,' 'multifaceted,' 'paradigm shift,' and 'plays a crucial role.' These phrases appear at much higher rates in AI output than human writing.

faqs.q: How can writers avoid being falsely flagged as AI?
faqs.a: Write naturally with varied sentence structure, avoid filler phrases and excessive transition words, use specific examples instead of general statements, and maintain your natural voice. Ironically, the same practices that make writing better also make it harder to mistake for AI.

faqs.q: What is word frequency analysis used for?
faqs.a: Word frequency analysis counts how often each word appears in a text, revealing vocabulary diversity, overused words, and writing patterns. It's used in linguistics, authorship attribution, plagiarism detection, SEO optimization, and increasingly in AI text detection.
